food_subgroup: Spiced rice porridge with chicken and coconut milk

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for Bubur Lambuk. Closest parent is rice-based congee or porridge with meat and spices (FOODON:03311069 — congee). No exact URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Spiced rice porridge with chicken and coconut milk (Malay bubur lambuk style) — closest parent match under rice-based porridge category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Bubur Lambuk. A00PC used as closest defensible parent for rice porridge with meat.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Bubur Lambuk or Malay spiced rice porridge.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Bubur Lambuk as served at Masjid Sultan during Ramadan is approximately 350g, providing around 287 kcal, 18.2g protein, 9.8g fat, and 37.8g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Bubur Lambuk — HPB Singapore Foodo; cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Malaysian Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Institute for Medical Research (IMR), Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Bubur Lambuk, cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD for validation. Both are approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas source policy.

gi_source.title: Rice porridge (congee) GI — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to Bubur Lambuk
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bubur Lambuk. GI of 75 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice porridge / congee, which has a documented high GI due to fully hydrated and gelatinised starch from extended cooking. The inclusion of protein (chicken/beef), fat (coconut milk), and aromatic spices in Bubur Lambuk may modestly attenuate the glycaemic response compared to plain congee, but the base GI remains high. GL per 350g serving calculated as (75 × 37.8g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 28.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 295mg per 100g; a standard 350g bowl delivers approximately 1,033mg sodium, representing 45% of the 2,300mg daily limit
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Bubur Lambuk to one bowl (350g) per meal and avoid adding additional salt, soy sauce, or high-sodium condiments; the dish itself is relatively lower in sodium than many other Malay dishes but a full bowl still contributes substantially to daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (~75) due to fully hydrated and gelatinised rice starch from prolonged simmering, resulting in rapid postprandial glucose elevation despite modest absolute carbohydrate content (37.8g per 350g bowl)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should consume Bubur Lambuk in reduced portions (150–200g), pair it with additional protein (egg, extra chicken) to slow gastric emptying, monitor postprandial blood glucose, and avoid consuming it as the sole carbohydrate source during Ramadan iftar or suhoor.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat content (1.8g per 100g) and moderate overall fat (2.8g per 100g); anti-inflammatory spices including ginger, cinnamon, and cardamom may confer modest cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bubur Lambuk is generally suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease due to its low saturated fat profile and anti-inflammatory spice content, but sodium intake from a full 350g bowl (approximately 1,033mg) should be factored into daily cardiovascular dietary management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, lemongrass, cinnamon, cardamom) and low saturated fat content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Bubur Lambuk is well-tolerated for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the warming anti-inflammatory spices and easily digestible texture make it a suitable comfort food during flares.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bubur Lambuk is a fragrant, slow-simmered Malay spiced rice porridge that holds deep cultural and religious significance in Singapore and Malaysia, particularly during the holy month of Ramadan. It is most famously distributed free to the public every evening from the historic Masjid Sultan mosque in Kampong Glam, Singapore, and from mosques across Malaysia. The porridge is prepared by simmering white rice to a silky, thick consistency with a complex blend of aromatic spices — typically lemongrass, ginger, cinnamon, cardamom, cloves, star anise, black pepper, cumin, and fenugreek — along with shredded chicken or beef, coconut milk, shallots, and garlic. It is served topped with crispy fried shallots, sliced spring onion, and Chinese celery. Unlike plain Chinese congee, Bubur Lambuk is distinctly fragrant and warming. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie dish at 82 kcal per 100g, with moderate protein (5.2g), low fat (2.8g), and moderate carbohydrate (10.8g). Despite its modest caloric density, the fully gelatinised rice starch from extended cooking gives it a high glycaemic index of approximately 75, making it a significant consideration for patients with type 2 diabetes consuming it during Ramadan fasting periods. Sodium content is 295mg per 100g, and a standard 350g bowl delivers approximately 1,033mg sodium. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary approved source for Singapore dishes) cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD (primary approved source for Malaysian dishes). Both are approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas source policy. GI derived from University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for rice porridge/congee, which is the closest defensible approved GI source.
notes: Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace per HPB Foodo source for this dish formulation. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values not reported by source; set to -1. Water content not reported by source; set to -1. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on rice porridge/congee from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Bubur Lambuk exists in the published literature. GL per serving calculated as (75 × 37.8g) / 100 = 28.35, rounded to 28.4. Copper (0.08mg), manganese (0.12mg), and vitamin K (2.5mcg) values are from enriched_json source with confidence 0.9. The dish is traditionally made with beef or chicken; the nutrient profile reflects a chicken-based version as the more common Singapore preparation. Country of origin is noted as both Singapore (Masjid Sultan distribution) and Malaysia (Kampung Baru, Kuala Lumpur origin); the dish is shared cultural heritage of both nations.
